stem: Concentric muscle contraction involves:
options: Muscle shortening while generating force (force overcomes resistance)
rationale: Concentric: muscle shortens as it generates force exceeding external resistance. Example: biceps curl lifting phase. Less force production than eccentric at same velocity.

stem: Eccentric muscle contraction involves:
options: Muscle lengthening while generating force (resistance overcomes muscle force, controlled lowering)
rationale: Eccentric: muscle lengthens under tension (controlled lowering). Higher force production than concentric at same velocity. More tissue damage → greater strength gains and DOMS.

stem: Isometric contraction occurs when:
options: Muscle generates force without change in muscle length or joint angle
rationale: Isometric: force generated without length change (joint angle constant). Strengthens at specific angle (± 10-20°). Used when motion is painful/contraindicated. Example: wall sit.

stem: The rotator cuff consists of:
options: Supraspinatus, infraspinatus, teres minor, subscapularis (SITS muscles)
rationale: SITS: Supraspinatus (abduction initiation, compression), Infraspinatus (ER), Teres minor (ER), Subscapularis (IR). All compress humeral head into glenoid for dynamic stability.

stem: The ACL prevents:
options: Anterior tibial translation and excessive knee extension and rotation
rationale: ACL: primary restraint to anterior tibial translation (85% of restraining force). Also restrains excessive knee extension, tibial IR, and valgus/varus at full extension.

stem: The PCL prevents:
options: Posterior tibial translation (strongest knee ligament, 'dashboard injury')
rationale: PCL: primary restraint to posterior tibial translation. Strongest intraarticular knee ligament. Dashboard injury mechanism. Posterior sag sign positive with PCL tear.

stem: The MCL prevents:
options: Excessive valgus (medial opening) stress at the knee
rationale: MCL: primary restraint to valgus stress (medial knee opening). Most commonly injured knee ligament. Often associated with ACL and medial meniscus injuries (unhappy triad).

stem: The unhappy triad (O'Donoghue triad) involves injury to:
options: ACL, MCL, and medial meniscus (though lateral meniscus may be more common than traditionally taught)
rationale: O'Donoghue triad: ACL + MCL + meniscus (medial or lateral). Caused by valgus force with foot planted. Combined injury common in contact sports.

stem: Wallenberg syndrome results from occlusion of the:
options: Posterior inferior cerebellar artery (PICA) or vertebral artery
rationale: Wallenberg (lateral medullary syndrome): PICA or vertebral artery occlusion. Ipsilateral: facial pain/temp loss, Horner syndrome, ataxia. Contralateral: body pain/temp loss. Dysphagia, vertigo.

stem: Middle cerebral artery stroke typically causes:
options: Contralateral face and arm weakness (more than leg), possible aphasia if dominant hemisphere, neglect if non-dominant
rationale: MCA stroke: contralateral hemiplegia (face/arm > leg), homonymous hemianopia, dominant hemisphere: aphasia, non-dominant: neglect/spatial deficits. Most common stroke territory.

stem: Anterior cerebral artery stroke typically causes:
options: Contralateral leg weakness greater than arm, personality changes, incontinence
rationale: ACA stroke: contralateral LE weakness > UE (opposite of MCA pattern). Personality/behavioral changes, incontinence, abulia (lack of initiative). Less common than MCA stroke.

stem: A patient with a left MCA stroke has Broca aphasia. This means:
options: They have non-fluent speech production but relatively intact comprehension
rationale: Broca (expressive/non-fluent) aphasia: impaired speech production, telegraphic speech, effortful output. Comprehension relatively preserved. Patient aware of deficit and may be frustrated.

stem: Wernicke aphasia is characterized by:
options: Fluent but meaningless speech (word salad), impaired comprehension, often unaware of deficit
rationale: Wernicke (receptive/fluent) aphasia: fluent but meaningless speech, poor comprehension, paraphasias, neologisms. Patient often unaware of deficit. Left posterior temporal lobe.

stem: Residual volume is:
options: Air remaining in the lungs after maximal exhalation (cannot be voluntarily exhaled)
rationale: Residual volume: ~1200 mL. Air remaining after maximal expiration. Cannot be directly measured by spirometry (requires body plethysmography or gas dilution). Increased in COPD (air trapping).

stem: Tidal volume is:
options: Maximum air that can be inhaled
The volume of air inhaled or exhaled during normal quiet breathing (~500 mL)
rationale: Tidal volume: ~500 mL at rest. Normal quiet breathing volume. Dead space: ~150 mL (anatomical + physiological). Alveolar ventilation = (TV - dead space) × RR.

stem: Vital capacity equals:
options: Tidal volume + inspiratory reserve volume + expiratory reserve volume (maximum air exchanged in one breath)
rationale: VC = TV + IRV + ERV. Maximum volume of air that can be exhaled after maximal inhalation. Decreased in both restrictive and obstructive disease. ~4800 mL in young adults.

stem: FEV1/FVC ratio less than 70% indicates:
options: Obstructive lung disease (air trapping prevents normal expiratory flow)
rationale: FEV1/FVC <0.70 (70%): obstructive pattern. FEV1 reduced more than FVC due to expiratory limitation. Severity: mild (FEV1 >80%), moderate (50-80%), severe (30-50%), very severe (<30%).

stem: The GMFM (Gross Motor Function Measure) is used for children with:
options: Cerebral palsy (88-item assessment of gross motor function across 5 dimensions)
rationale: GMFM: gold standard gross motor assessment for CP. 88 items (or 66-item version) across 5 dimensions: lying/rolling, sitting, crawling/kneeling, standing, walking/running/jumping.

stem: GMFCS Level I for CP means:
options: Walks without limitations in community (may have quality limitations in advanced motor skills)
rationale: GMFCS I: walks without limitations. May have limitations in advanced skills (running, jumping). Level II: walks with limitations. III: walks with assistive device. IV: self-mobility with limitations. V: transported in wheelchair.

stem: GMFCS Level V for CP means:
options: Transported in manual wheelchair — severely limited self-mobility even with assistive technology
rationale: GMFCS V: severely limited voluntary movement control. Transported in manual wheelchair by others. Powered mobility may be possible with extensive adaptations. Focus on positioning, comfort, participation.

stem: A child with spastic diplegia CP MOST affects:
options: Lower extremities more than upper extremities (bilateral LE involvement)
rationale: Spastic diplegia: bilateral LE involvement > UE. Most common CP type in premature infants (periventricular leukomalacia). Scissoring gait pattern common. May walk with assistive devices.

stem: Stage III pressure injury involves:
options: Full-thickness tissue loss with visible subcutaneous fat but not bone/tendon/muscle
rationale: Stage III: full-thickness skin loss. Subcutaneous fat visible but bone, tendon, muscle not exposed. May include undermining/tunneling. Depth varies by anatomical location.

stem: Stage IV pressure injury involves:
options: Full-thickness tissue loss with exposed bone, tendon, or muscle
rationale: Stage IV: full-thickness tissue loss with exposed bone, tendon, or muscle. May include undermining/tunneling. Often requires surgical intervention. High risk for osteomyelitis.

stem: Deep tissue pressure injury (DTPI) presents as:
options: Intact skin with persistent non-blanchable deep red, maroon, or purple discoloration or blood-filled blister
rationale: DTPI: intact or non-intact skin with localized non-blanchable deep red/maroon/purple discoloration. Damage originates at bone-tissue interface. May evolve rapidly to full-thickness.

stem: An unstageable pressure injury has:
options: Full-thickness tissue loss with wound bed obscured by slough and/or eschar (unable to determine depth)
rationale: Unstageable: full-thickness loss but base obscured by slough (yellow/tan/gray/green) or eschar (tan/brown/black). Cannot determine true depth until debrided. Stable eschar on heels: do not debride.

stem: Negative pressure wound therapy (wound VAC) is contraindicated with:
options: Untreated osteomyelitis, necrotic tissue with eschar, malignancy in wound, exposed vessels/organs
rationale: NPWT contraindications: untreated osteomyelitis, necrotic tissue requiring debridement first, malignancy in wound bed, exposed blood vessels/organs, fistulas to organs, active bleeding.

stem: Type 1 diabetes differs from Type 2 in that Type 1:
options: Is autoimmune destruction of beta cells requiring insulin replacement (usually childhood onset)
rationale: Type 1: autoimmune beta-cell destruction → absolute insulin deficiency. Requires insulin. Usually childhood/adolescent onset. Type 2: insulin resistance ± relative deficiency. Usually adult onset.

stem: The ceiling effect in outcome measures means:
options: Patients at the high end of function cannot show further improvement on the measure (scores cluster at maximum)
rationale: Ceiling effect: measure cannot discriminate improvement in high-functioning patients (scores at maximum). Floor effect: cannot discriminate among low-functioning patients (scores at minimum).

stem: A randomized controlled trial (RCT) is considered the gold standard for:
options: Determining cause-and-effect relationship between intervention and outcome
rationale: RCT: random allocation to groups controls for confounders. Double-blinding reduces bias. Best design for establishing causation. Systematic review of RCTs = highest evidence level.

stem: Medicare Part B covers outpatient PT under:
options: Medical insurance covering outpatient therapy services with annual therapy caps/thresholds
rationale: Medicare Part B: outpatient therapy services. Annual payment threshold triggers review. Requires medical necessity documentation. Plan of care must be certified by physician/NPP.

stem: A PTA must report to the supervising PT when:
options: When there is a change in patient status, new findings, or the patient is not progressing as expected
rationale: PTA must communicate with PT: change in patient status, new symptoms, failure to progress, safety concerns, patient questions beyond PTA scope, and per state law requirements.

stem: The NPTE-PTA exam consists of:
options: 200 scored questions (plus 50 pretest questions) over 4 hours
rationale: NPTE-PTA: 200 scored + 50 pretest (not identified) = 250 total questions. 4 hours. Computer-based. Passing score: criterion-referenced (scaled score 600). Content per FSBPT blueprint.
